guides.text: Let's first understand the goal. $I \to I$ means '$I$ implies $I$' — if $I$ is true, then $I$ is true. Hardly controversial.
To prove an implication, we assume the left-hand side and try to reach the right-hand side. So let's assume $I$.

The symbol $\vdash$ is pronounced 'entails'. Everything to its left is the **context** — a set of propositions we're allowed to take for granted. The right-hand side is what we can conclude from them.
So $\{I\} \vdash I$ reads: 'given $I$, we can deduce $I$'. Which is just a fancy way of saying: if you know $I$, you know $I$.
Now, '$A$ implies $B$' **means** that $B$ follows from assuming $A$. We already have $\{I\} \vdash I$. Use `->-intro` to discharge the assumption and close the proof:

unlocks.name: assume
unlocks.description: If you assume a proposition, you can deduce it — because you just said it's true.

unlocks.name: →-intro
unlocks.description: If from assuming $p$ you can reach $q$, then $p$ implies $q$ — and you can safely drop $p$ from the context.
